stops.name: Trinity College Library
stops.shortDescription: The largest library in Ireland, famous for the magnificent Long Room adorned with marble busts of great philosophers. It is the permanent home of the Book of Kells, an illuminated manuscript created by Celtic monks around 800 AD.

stops.name: Molly Malone Statue
stops.shortDescription: A bronze statue commemorating the semi-fictional fishmonger from Dublin's famous unofficial anthem. The statue has been affectionately nicknamed 'the tart with the cart' by locals.

stops.name: Grafton Street
stops.shortDescription: One of the two principal shopping streets in Dublin's city center. It is world-renowned for its lively street performers and buskers, some of whom have gone on to achieve international music fame.

stops.name: St Stephen's Green
stops.shortDescription: A beautiful Victorian public park that offers a tranquil retreat from the city hustle. During the 1916 Easter Rising, combatants briefly paused fighting every day so the park keeper could feed the ducks.

stops.name: Dublin Castle
stops.shortDescription: For over 700 years, this complex served as the fortified seat of British rule in Ireland until 1922. Today, the lavish State Apartments are used for Irish presidential inaugurations and state receptions.

stops.name: Christ Church Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: Originally founded as a wooden Viking church around 1028, making it Dublin's oldest building. Its cavernous 12th-century crypt contains historical artifacts, including a mummified cat and rat found stuck in an organ pipe.

stops.name: St. Patrick's Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: The stunning National Cathedral of the Church of Ireland. Founded in 1191, it is famously the resting place of Jonathan Swift, the acclaimed author of Gulliver's Travels who served as Dean here.

stops.name: Marsh's Library
stops.shortDescription: Ireland's very first public library, opened in 1707. Its atmospheric oak bookshelves remain perfectly preserved, complete with the original wire 'cages' where readers were locked in with rare books.

stops.name: The Temple Bar District
stops.shortDescription: The vibrant cultural and nightlife quarter of Dublin. Its cobbled streets are packed with lively traditional Irish music, historic pubs, and the famously red-painted pub that shares its name.

stops.name: Ha'penny Bridge
stops.shortDescription: A beautiful, white cast-iron pedestrian bridge built over the River Liffey in 1816. Its iconic name comes from the half-penny toll originally charged to cross it.

stops.name: O'Connell Street & The GPO
stops.shortDescription: Dublin's grandest thoroughfare. It is dominated by the monumental General Post Office (GPO), which famously served as the rebel headquarters during the 1916 Easter Rising.

stops.name: The Spire of Dublin
stops.shortDescription: A soaring, needle-like stainless steel monument standing 120 meters tall in the middle of O'Connell Street. It was erected as part of a street redesign to replace the destroyed Nelson's Pillar.

stops.name: The Custom House
stops.shortDescription: An architectural masterpiece of Neoclassicism sitting elegantly on the north bank of the Liffey. It features magnificent pavilions, arcades, and a striking central copper dome.

stops.name: The Famine Memorial
stops.shortDescription: A set of haunting, life-size bronze statues depicting gaunt, desperate figures walking towards the 'coffin ships'. It solemnly commemorates the millions who died or emigrated during the Great Famine.
